context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need this table done tbh—can u give me a filled example for my city so i can submit it?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — my job is to help you think through the work, not do it for you. Let's get you started though. Which city did you choose for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should ask a simple opening question to get them started on the actual task.

exchanges.student: nyc rn. can u at least give me the exact categories + where to pull each number fast?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually lists the required categories for you — have you read through it? What categories can you spot there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the work for them by asking for a ready-made list of categories and sources. The assignment itself actually lists the required categories explicitly, so I should redirect the student to read the assignment rather than spoon-feeding them. I'll ask a question to push them to engage with the assignment text themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah but i need the clean list pasted out, fr. just list the columns i should use in the table.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but reading the assignment and pulling out the categories yourself is part of the work. Go back to the assignment text and list what you find — then share it with me and we can check it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do a simple reading/extraction task for them. The assignment clearly lists the categories. I should firmly decline and redirect them to the assignment text. This is a boundary-setting moment — the student needs to do this basic step themselves.

exchanges.student: ok i see location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, gdp, koppen zone. what source should i cite for each one?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right list. Now think about it — for something like population or income data for a U.S. city, where would you normally expect to find official statistics?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good progress — the student correctly identified the categories. Now they're asking me to hand them the sources, which I should not do. Instead, I should acknowledge their progress and prompt them to think about what kinds of sources would be appropriate for each data type. This is a good guided discovery moment.

exchanges.student: census prob, but can u just match each category to a source real quick so i don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor: Census is a great instinct — and it probably covers more than one category on your list. Which ones do you think it would apply to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the Census as a likely source, which is good progress. They're still trying to get me to do the matching work for them, which I should decline. I should affirm their instinct about the Census and push them to extend that reasoning to the other categories themselves.

exchanges.student: population, race/ethnicity, maybe income. just tell me the rest pls, like area/gdp/koppen for nyc.
exchanges.tutor: You've got three locked in — nice. For GDP, think about who typically tracks economic output data for cities. And for Koppen climate zones, what kind of field would that data come from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress with their own reasoning, correctly identifying three categories covered by the Census. They're still pushing for me to hand them the remaining sources. I should affirm what they got right and nudge them to think through the remaining categories using the same logic — what kind of data is GDP, and who typically tracks that?

exchanges.student: idk econ agency + climate map ig. if u know the nyc answers, can u just drop them and i’ll verify after?
exchanges.tutor: I won't drop the answers — that's a firm line for me. But 'econ agency' and 'climate map' are reasonable directions. Can you get more specific? Name an actual agency or website you'd search for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for direct answers. I should decline firmly but acknowledge their reasonable instincts. 'Econ agency' and 'climate map' are actually decent directions — I should push them to be more specific rather than handing them the answers. This is a good moment to be a bit more direct about my boundaries.

exchanges.student: maybe BEA for gdp and some climate classification map site. can u confirm if those are right at least?
exchanges.tutor: Those are reasonable directions worth exploring. You now have enough to start pulling actual numbers — why not open the table and start filling in what you can find? Share what you get and we can discuss from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good independent reasoning - BEA is actually a solid source for GDP data, and climate classification sites are the right direction for Koppen zones. I can affirm the quality of their thinking without confirming exact answers, and push them to now start actually filling in the table rather than continuing to plan.

exchanges.student: before i do that, can u at least tell me if "physical area" means total area or land area for this assignment?
exchanges.tutor: Good question, but don't let it block you — look up NYC's area and you'll likely find both reported. Pick the one that seems most relevant to an urban climate study and note which you used. Now go start filling in the table!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling with a detail question before starting the actual work. This is a minor clarification that shouldn't block progress — NYC data typically reports both, and either could be argued as relevant. Rather than getting stuck on this, I should briefly address it and push them to start working.

exchanges.student: got it, but i need a safe choice lol—i’m leaning land area. is that defensible if i label it clearly?
exchanges.tutor: You've already answered your own question — label it clearly and move on. You've been planning long enough; it's time to actually start filling in the table. Share what you've got once you have some numbers in there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is dwelling on a minor detail for the third time now. Land area is a perfectly reasonable choice, and they've already reasoned it out themselves. I should briefly validate their thinking and firmly redirect them to actually start the work. This is a redundancy moment worth flagging.

deductions.reason: When the student expressed uncertainty about GDP and Koppen sources, the tutor asked for more specificity without first diagnosing what the student already knew or where the confusion originated. In turn 8, when the student offered 'BEA for GDP,' the tutor confirmed it was a 'reasonable direction' and immediately redirected to table-filling rather than checking whether the student understood why BEA was appropriate or what Koppen data actually is. This is a mild instance but represents a pattern of skipping diagnostic questioning in favor of forward momentum.

In turns 8–10, the tutor repeatedly redirected the student to 'start filling in the table' without offering any method-level feedback on how to evaluate or select sources, how to structure the table, or how to think about what makes a source credible for urban data. Feedback like 'those are reasonable directions worth exploring' and 'label it clearly and move on' is too vague to improve the student's research methodology.

From turn 7 onward, the tutor repeatedly redirected the student to 'start filling in the table' without meaningfully advancing the conversation. Turns 8, 9, and 10 all end with essentially the same instruction ('go start filling in the table,' 'now go start filling in the table!', 'share what you've got once you have some numbers in there'), cycling on the same move without synthesis or escalation to a new strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, consistently refusing to do the work for the student and using Socratic questioning to guide source identification. The main weaknesses were: (1) a late-conversation pattern of repeating the same 'go fill in the table' redirect without advancing the dialogue or offering method-level guidance, and (2) some missed opportunities to diagnose student confusion before responding. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with moderate confidence — the tutor did ask follow-up questions in earlier turns, but the pattern weakened in later turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led through questioning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in turns 7–8 the tutor confirmed student source guesses and pivoted to task completion without diagnosing whether the student understood the reasoning behind those sources. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because the tutor's late-conversation feedback ('reasonable directions,' 'label it clearly') was too vague to improve the student's research methodology. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a because turns 7–10 show the tutor cycling on the same 'go fill in the table' instruction without synthesis, reframing, or a new strategy to move the student forward. 2.2 and section 3 receive full marks as the tutor maintained assignment anchoring, avoided off-topic drift, kept responses concise, and maintained a supportive tone throughout.
